approach.reasoning:
Overall the agent used a reasonable, data-driven approach: it identified EIA utility IDs for Xcel subsidiaries, called tariff APIs/get_utility_tariffs for the relevant utilities and states, and attempted to filter tariffs by peak-kW applicability and extract demand-charge information. These steps align with the suggested approach of using OpenEI tariff data to find commercial classes applicable to >500 kW and to extract demand charges.

However there are notable gaps and some flawed choices that reduce the approach quality: 1) Filtering logic is weak/ambiguous — the agent used the condition "peakkwcapacitymax > 500 and peakkwcapacitymin <= 5000", which neither cleanly selects tariffs applicable to customers with demand >500 kW nor reliably isolates commercial classes whose minimum threshold exceeds 500 kW. The correct logic should explicitly include tariffs where min_kW >= 500 (or whose range includes >500) and carefully handle open-ended max/min values. 2) The agent mixes two API strategies (direct OpenEI HTTP requests with a DEMO_KEY and toolkit get_utility_tariffs calls) and does not show reconciliation of differing outputs or error handling for inconsistent/missing fields. 3) The agent extracts demand rates but appears to conflate different demand-rate fields (flatdemandrate, demandratestructure, flatdemandstructure) without standardizing units, time basis, or identifying whether rates are $/kW-month vs $/kW-day etc., which is necessary for valid cross-state comparisons. 4) The mapping of EIA IDs to states/utilities is partially reused for multiple states (reusing single EIA IDs for several states) without justification; some Xcel subsidiaries serve multiple states but the agent did not verify that each tariff returned maps specifically to the named state or service territory. 5) The workflow stops at listing tariff excerpts rather than computing comparable summary metrics (e.g., maximum demand charge per state, normalized $/kW-month), which was central to the question.

Tool usage was mostly appropriate (using tariffs API/tool), but the agent should have used clearer selection criteria, normalized rate measures, verified state-to-tariff mappings, and produced comparative summaries. These shortcomings justify a rating of 3 (notable gaps) rather than higher.

accuracy.reasoning: I compared each expected attribute to the agent's answer. The agent listed the required states (plus an extra mention of New Mexico), correctly identified the relevant service classes and produced approximate $/kW values that fall within the stated tolerances (<=2 absolute or <=10% relative). The agent's overall conclusion that Texas has the highest peak demand charge matches the expected summary. The one mismatch is applicability/thresholds: the canonical expectation specifies Texas >25 kW and WI/MI mandatory condition tied to 15-min demand ≥200 kW for 4 of 12 months; the agent omitted the Texas >25 kW threshold, gave an incomplete WI/MI temporal condition, and introduced a >1,000 kW mandatory TOD statement for MN/ND/SD that does not match the expected wording. Therefore 4 of 5 attributes match, yielding an alignment score of 0.8.

sources.reasoning: The agent claims to have used the OpenEI Utility Rate Database (URDB), which is an appropriate and authoritative source for tariff schedules and is consistent with the suggested data source. However, the answer does not include explicit citations, tariff IDs, tariff names/versions, effective dates, or links to the specific OpenEI/URDB entries used, so the provenance of the specific numeric charge values cannot be verified. Several other issues reduce validity: (1) The agent mixes subsidiaries and states imprecisely (e.g., combining Minnesota/ND/SD under a single Northern States Power Co. - Minnesota entry without listing the separate company identifiers or tariff numbers), (2) The schedules named (e.g., SST, PST, TTOU, A14/A17, Cg-9.1) are presented without official tariff references or confirmation that those schedules apply to >500 kW customers, (3) No effective dates or riders (DSM, transmission, fuel, taxes) are explicitly sourced despite being cited as adjustments, and (4) some numeric values (notably the high SPS/Texas summer values and specific adjustment magnitudes) appear precise but lack any supporting citation and could be out-of-date or aggregated from different tariff versions. Overall the choice of OpenEI URDB is appropriate, but the absence of explicit references, the lack of documented methodology (how >500 kW classes were filtered), and possible conflation of territories and schedules mean the answer is not sufficiently verifiable. To reach a higher validity score the agent should list the exact OpenEI/URDB tariff entries or EIA utility IDs used, include effective dates, and show which service classes were filtered for demand >500 kW and how seasonal/on-peak definitions were applied.

attribute_details.name: States served by Xcel (in dataset)
attribute_details.expected: Texas, Wisconsin, Michigan, Colorado, Minnesota, North Dakota, South Dakota
attribute_details.agent_value: Texas, Wisconsin, Michigan, Colorado, Minnesota, North Dakota, South Dakota (also mentioned New Mexico)

attribute_details.name: Service class with highest applicable peak-demand charge by state/region
attribute_details.expected: Texas: Secondary General Service; Wisconsin/Michigan: Cg-9.1 Large General Time-of-Day Secondary Mandatory; Colorado: Secondary Standby Service (SST); Minnesota/North Dakota/South Dakota: Multiple General Service classes (largest applicable general service)
attribute_details.agent_value: Texas: Secondary General Service; Wisconsin/Michigan: Cg-9.1 Large General TOD Secondary; Colorado: Schedule SST (secondary standby-style); MN/ND/SD: general service TOD schedules (A14/A17), largest applicable general service

attribute_details.name: Approximate demand charge ($/kW) by state/region
attribute_details.expected: Texas: ≈ $23/kW; Wisconsin/Michigan: ≈ $17/kW; Colorado: ≈ $20/kW; Minnesota/North Dakota/South Dakota: ≈ $17/kW
attribute_details.agent_value: Texas: summer secondary up to ~$22.4/kW (range shown up to $22.73) → ~23; WI/MI: on-peak ~$13/kW plus ~$3.75/kW distribution ≈ $16.75 (~17); Colorado: summer secondary ~$15.15 + adjustments ~$4.91 ≈ $20.06; MN/ND/SD: summer secondary ~$16.49 + ~$0.32 adj ≈ $16.8 (~17)

attribute_details.name: Applicability / mandatory thresholds for large commercial customers
attribute_details.expected: Texas: applies to >25 kW secondary customers (but excludes resale/shared service and oil/natural gas production); Wisconsin/Michigan: mandatory for customers with 15-min demand ≥200 kW for 4 of 12 months; other regions: multiple riders and thresholds, customer-specific enrollment rules
attribute_details.agent_value: States: agent stated WI/MI mandatory for >200 kW; Minnesota/ND/SD mandatory TOD for >1,000 kW in some language; Texas thresholds not specified as >25 kW (agent focused on large C&I and sometimes >500 kW)

attribute_details.name: Overall comparative conclusion
attribute_details.expected: Texas shows the highest peak demand charge among the regions in the OpenEI tariff data
attribute_details.agent_value: Texas (and in the agent's write-up Texas/New Mexico SPS territory) shows the highest base capacity charges
